title: Prismatic Trust: How Structural and Behavioral Signals in Networks Explain Trust Accumulation
abstract: The predominant focus of the organizational literature on trust has been on direct interactions between actors. Whereas this emphasis has solidified our understanding of the dyadic foundations of trust, we know relatively little about the mechanisms of trust creation in network contexts. In this paper, we introduce the network mechanism of prismatic trust to explain why some actors are more trusted than others. Specifically, we posit that networks act as prisms that generate signals of trustworthiness based on not only actors’ positions in the social structure, but also their networking behavior. Moreover, we also theorize that the combination of signals from network structure and behavior amplifies trust accumulation in network actors. We test our predictions using data from an online social trading platform with more than 28,000 traders across 38 weeks. We find that traders who occupy positions of higher status in the network and those who express positive sentiments in the content of their communications (networking behaviors), accumulate more trustors. Furthermore, the positive effects of network status and the expression of positive sentiments on trust accumulation are mutually reinforcing. In sum, we contribute to the organizational literature on trust by proposing the role of a prismatic view in explaining how trust accumulates in network actors as a function of their position in social structure, their networking behavior, and a combination of the two.

author.Aks Zaheer: Carlson School of Management, University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55455
author.Bill McEvily: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3E6, Canada
author.Mani Subramani: Carlson School of Management, University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55455

article.Introduction: Why are some actors within a network more trusted than others? In the organizational literature, trust has typically been considered as an outcome of direct dyadic experience between a trustor and trustee (hereafter the relational perspective), be it interpersonal, interunit, or interorganizational (e.g.,Mayer et al. 1995,McAllister 1995,Rousseau et al. 1998,Zaheer et al. 1998,Schilke et al. 2021). Whereas the mechanisms of trust creation have been extensively studied in direct, dyadic relationships, we know relatively little about the mechanisms of trust creation in network settings, which consist of systems of connections that extend beyond dyads (e.g.,Granovetter 1985,Burt et al. 2022,Vanneste and Gulati 2022). We maintain that networks represent a vast space beyond direct and indirect connections among actors that enables trust based on a fundamentally different set of mechanisms. Specifically, we employ network theory to argue that some actors are trusted more than others by virtue of observable signals of trustworthiness emanating from their network positions and networking behavior. In this way, networks act as prisms (Podolny 2001) that generate trust. We further argue that developing such a perspective of trust creation within networks warrants enhanced attention given the evolution of personal and professional networks into digital spaces in which not only is trust important, but information for making trust judgments is also ubiquitous. For instance, observable cues, such as online profiles, followers, ratings, and posts on platforms such as LinkedIn and Facebook, are precursors to gauging trust prior to initiating a wide range of interactions and exchanges, such as deciding whether to meet someone, hire a person, or even purchase goods and services. Digital platforms and online communities enable this observability in a way that traditional networks cannot (Hampton and Wellman 2001). Here, we study an online social trading network in which trusting other actors, especially those with whom one has neither a direct nor indirect connection, is highly consequential. We explain why some actors in the network are more widely trusted than others based on the observable signals that the network refracts, like a prism, which we refer to as the prismatic view of trust—hereafter prismatic trust. Taken together, we address the following research question: in networks in which ties and behaviors are observable, how do network actors accumulate trustors in the absence of direct or indirect connections to them? Focusing on network signals, we advance a theory of prismatic trust that goes beyond relational mechanisms of trust based on direct, dyadic experience between a trustor and trustee (e.g.,Mayer et al. 1995). We define prismatic trust as a trustor’s willingness to be vulnerable to a trustee, in the absence of a direct or indirect connection, based on structural and behavioral signals from the network(s) to which they both belong. From a structural perspective, an actor’s network position serves as a prism that projects signals to other members about the actor’s underlying qualities (Podolny 2001,Burt et al. 2013). Critically, such a structural signal derives from the actor’s observed status in terms of being connected to prestigious members of the network (Podolny 1993). We argue that an actor’s status signals the actor’s trustworthiness in terms of competence and expertise. From a behavioral perspective, the positive sentiments embodied in an actor’s observable network interactions signal other elements of the actor’s underlying trustworthiness, specifically, benevolence and goodwill. In addition to the independent effects, we further theorize that the structural (status) and behavioral (positive sentiments) signals operate jointly, in tandem, such that trust is reinforced for actors who both occupy high status positions and express positive sentiments. Taken together, our theory of prismatic trust offers a distinct theoretical account of the generation of trust in network settings. Previous research on networks and trust has emphasized the pathways through which trust is generated, such as relational and structural embeddedness (Granovetter 1992) in the form of strong ties, reciprocity and transitive closure (Burt and Knez 1995;Ferrin et al. 2006;Wong and Boh 2010;Lusher et al. 2012,2014;Vanneste 2016;Jones and Shah 2021). The class of mechanisms we propose for trust within networks, however, are qualitatively different. The divergence is important because pathway-based theories are typically focused on explaining trust locally (e.g., directly connected triads), whereas our prismatic view addresses trust more distally and broadly, even among network actors who are disconnected. More importantly, the pathway and prismatic approaches speak to distinct theoretical mechanisms for explaining trust within networks. Whereas the pathway line of research is focused on the network as pipes through which content flows, our view focuses on the network as a prism (Podolny 2001,Burt et al. 2013) that processes not only structural, but also behavioral signals. We validate our theory of prismatic trust in the context of an online social trading platform we refer to as EZ-Trade (a pseudonym). We use data on more than 28,000 traders across 38 weeks on EZ-Trade and find compelling support for our core theoretical claims. Specifically, we observe that prismatic trust is predicated on a set of distinct mechanisms that draw on both traders’ structural positions and their networking behaviors. In particular, we show that both the focal actor’s status and the positive sentiments they express are generating prismatic trust. Moreover, the interplay of the focal actors’ structural position and their networking behaviors has mutually reinforcing effects on trust accumulation in the actors. We contribute to the organizational literature on networks and trust in several ways. First, we complement existing network pathway-based views with a novel view predicated on networks as prisms and further theorize the underlying mechanisms through which trust may emerge. We consider our theory of prismatic trust to be significant because it introduces a fundamentally novel network-based theory of trust that enhances scholarly understanding of how trust can be massively scaled up. By doing so, we shift the focus of trust research from the levels of trust in interpersonal relationships to the breadth or scale of trust in social systems. Our theory further allows us to examine how a network actor attracts multiple trustors, an outcome that has yet to be considered in previous research. Second, we contribute to the literature on networking behavior (Bensaou et al. 2014,Shipilov et al. 2014,Soda et al. 2018) by explaining how positive sentiments translate into trust within networks by virtue of a prismatic mechanism. In particular, public displays of positive sentiments have a strong influence on not only the parties directly involved, but also other members of the network who are neither directly nor indirectly connected to the trustee. In this way, we show how networking behaviors operate as salient, dynamic signals that enrich and complement the prismatic effect on trust beyond the static structural properties of the network. Thus, our focus on networking behavior contributes to the creation of an overarching framework that integrates structural features with behavioral elements (Giddens 1979), thereby providing a more comprehensive theory of trust within networks than prior treatments. Third, we contribute to the status literature by theorizing the role of network structural signals in explaining trust within networks. Because status is treated as a proxy for the unobservable quality of actors, high-status actors are viewed as more trustworthy (Podolny 2001). Whereas the status literature invokes the concept of trust (Podolny 1993), it has yet to examine this relationship systematically. The significance of status for trust in network settings is that, via the capacity to broadcast signals across the network, status provides a basis for impersonal trust (Shapiro 1987), rooted in collective judgments, and not otherwise accounted for by other mechanisms such as institutions (Granovetter 1985). From a managerial and organizational standpoint, our theory and results highlight the possibility of creating social spaces that generate observable signals aimed at enhancing the production and level of trust among organizational actors that are not in close proximity. Because prior work on trust in network contexts has yet to highlight the significance of publicness and observability, which is central to our theorizing about prismatic trust, in the next section we establish the significance of these concepts in our research context prior to developing our theoretical claims and predictions.
article.Trust in Online Social Trading: Online social trading began as early as 2003 and has been used as a context to study how social influence alters decision-making processes in financial markets (Pan et al. 2012). Social trading is best described as enabling the use of social networks, via a set of purposive directed ties among traders, to support trading decisions. Beyond enabling online trading of currencies, exchange-traded funds, market indices, commodities, stocks, and cryptocurrencies, the core features of social trading platforms include facilitation of communication among retail traders, publicly visible trades (to platform members), and observable trader performance as well as information on connections among traders. On the EZ-Trade platform, traders can choose either or both of two trading strategies, solo trade and copy trade. With solo trade, the trader operates autonomously on the platform. With copy trade, the trader chooses one or more other traders (by autonomously creating a “copy tie”) whose subsequent trading actions are copied onto the portfolio of the copying trader.1It is worth pointing out that copy trading occurs at the trader level rather than the transaction level. Once traders decide to copy another trader, they are entrusting a proportion of their portfolio to all transactions undertaken by the copied trader. We view this decision as a clear expression of trust because of the copying trader’s (the trustor’s) acceptance of risk and vulnerability to the actions of the copied trader (the trustee).2The more a focal trader is copied by a larger number of traders, the greater the amount of trust bestowed upon the focal trader by the network—what we refer to as trust accumulation. In addition to copy-trading ties, there are two further types of potential connections among traders on the platform: information seeking in the form of following (the basis for network structure) and communication (the basis for networking behavior). Note that, in the aggregate, ties of copy trading, information seeking, and communication each constitute a trader-by-trader network. Information Seeking Traders are able to seek information on others’ trading actions by publicly following their activity on the platform. When following others, traders are instantly informed about the trading actions of followed traders through a feed. It is important to note that the decision to follow a trader is a separate feature from the decision to copy a trader on the platform. Followers differ from copiers in that followers are instantly provided information on the investment activities of traders they are following, but the investment decisions of traders they are following are not replicated on the followers’ portfolios unlike the investment decisions of copied traders. Moreover, EZ-Trade allows any member to view any other member’s profile, on which the count and names of followers are displayed. Further, clicking through on the name of a follower displays the count and names of the follower’s followers.3 Communication The platform also allows traders to communicate with one another but only through public text messages on discussion boards visible to all. Discussion boards are located on each trader’s profile page, and any trader can post communications on any other trader’s discussion board. Such directed communications often involve statements or questions about trends in the market and about specific investments, such as currencies or stocks (seeTable 1for examples). Beyond factual information, the content of communications also contains expressions of sentiments that are indicative of a trader’s affective appraisal (Ridgeway 1994). In this regard, the emotion that a trader expresses in this communication is a key networking behavior that affects how other traders perceive the focal trader. Table 1.Examples of Positive Sentiments on EZ-Trade Table 1.Examples of Positive Sentiments on EZ-Trade Note.The words considered to be associated with positive sentiments by LIWC are underlined, and typos have been left uncorrected to preserve authenticity. Taken together, the information seeking and communication ties among traders on EZ-Trade provide an excellent research context for examining the antecedents of prismatic trust. We argue that the networked nature of EZ-Trade provides the opportunity to study prismatic trust emerging from structural and behavioral signals. Specifically, we use the structure of the following network (status) together with traders’ networking behaviors (positive sentiments in communications) to explain trust accumulation in a focal trader (copy trading ties).
article.Theory: As a starting point for our theory development of prismatic trust, we clarify how its underlying mechanisms are fundamentally distinct relative to those of relational, network pathway, and institutional trust. Whereas mechanisms of relational trust are premised on direct experience and encapsulated within dyads and mechanisms of network pathway trust rely on flows of referrals and contagion through indirect connections, the mechanisms of prismatic trust are based on signals emanating from the network. Prismatic trust mechanisms are also distinct from those of institutional mechanisms (Granovetter 1985,Zucker 1986), which arise from “the safety one feels about a situation because of guarantees, safety nets, or structures” (McKnight et al. 1998, p. 478). Such mechanisms are diluted in the sense that the willingness to be vulnerable is promoted by institutional safeguards. In contrast, prismatic trust is grounded in structural and behavioral network signals about an actor’s trustworthiness.4 In developing the mechanism of prismatic trust, we theorize that the network structural and behavioral elements are observable to all members of the network. We emphasize that our theorizing on the observability of network status is entirely consistent with foundational work in the literature, fromPodolny’s (1994)study of investment banking syndicates, in which “tombstone” advertisements are publicly visible to all to theErtug and Castellucci (2013)study of NBA teams, in which MVP and All-Star awards information is widely available, among others.5Inherent to the notion of status is its publicness or the accessibility and observability of an actor’s affiliation to others. Likewise, we argue that networking behaviors are also publicly visible when members of the network are able to observe interactions among not only socially proximate, but also distal members of the network. Consistent with this idea,Jones and Shah (2021, p. 217) point out, “In many situations, the ties that a central actor possesses are observable in a broader social field. Thus, those not tied to central actors nor their advocates may develop trust simply by observing central actors’ ties.” In this way, publicness is a commonly observed characteristic of many social contexts and, accordingly, a fundamental property in our theorization of prismatic trust. Network Status as a Signal of Competence In the network context of online social trading, search is simultaneously facilitated and problematic. It is facilitated in the sense that information on traders’ network positions and their networking actions is readily available and abundant. It is problematic in the sense of identifying which actors possess valuable trading expertise. Such expertise entails predicting the trajectories of currencies, stocks, and commodities. Thus, the search problem (Hansen 1999) that precedes a copy trading decision is one of identifying traders who have the ability to make sense of market information in a way that others cannot and who are thereby able to capitalize on profitable trading opportunities. Identifying expert traders is challenging in a network of even modest size because of the costs of search and evaluation. We argue that traders face a problem of how to best allocate their time and effort in order to accurately identify capable others. Rather than being comprehensive, individuals take shortcuts and use heuristics (Tversky and Kahneman 1974). We propose that, in a network of publicly visible ties and behaviors, such as our research context, heuristics based on network signals are relevant. We argue that actor status in the information-seeking (following) network provides critical second order information on trader expertise. Moreover, the amount of attention a high-status trader attracts from other high-status traders reflects the trader’s position in the hierarchical status ordering of the network. We adopt network status to reflect the hierarchical ordering inherent among the traders being copied. Our conceptualization of status and its outcomes is consistent with that ofKilduff and Krackhardt (1994), who posit that relationships with high-status actors induce positive judgments and evaluations by other network members. We argue that status is an indicator of trustworthiness, drawing on the well-established association between status and the competence dimension of trustworthiness (Mayer et al. 1995). For example, in the introduction to the special issue ofOrganization Scienceon status, the editors (Chen et al. 2011, p. 302) highlight the fact that high status is associated with high perceived competence and, further that “…the causal direction, in fact, goes more from status to perceived competence than the other way around.” The disproportionate attention garnered by a high-status trader reflects the generalized confidence placed in the trader by other members of the network. Such generalized confidence is based on the confluence of individual evaluations of a focal trader’s ability to make sense of market information and trends. Such a dynamic makes capable actors highly visible and salient for traders who are seeking to identify prestigious traders. Critically, as distinct from firsthand evaluations, the status ordering is itself the basis upon which judgments about a focal trader’s ability are made. We treat a focal trader’s ascribed competence or ability as a core dimension of trustworthiness, consistent with prior conceptualizations of trust (Mayer et al. 1995). Traders are more likely to be willing to make themselves vulnerable to those traders who they judge as high in ability as indicated by their status. Because a focal trader’s status in the information-seeking network is publicly visible to all other traders in the network, trust accumulates in the form of other traders (trustors) copy trading the focal trader (trustee). Accordingly, we predict a network structure effect on trust accumulation as follows. The greater the focal trader’s status in the information-seeking network, the greater the focal trader’s trust accumulation. Networking Behavior as Cues of Goodwill Whereas the emphasis on structural features of networks, such as status, has occupied center stage in network research, a recent and growing stream of work focuses on the importance of networking behavior, and its combination with structure, to predict outcomes (e.g.,Carnabuci and Diószegi 2015,Soda et al. 2018). A core premise of this literature is that social structure and networking behavior are not isomorphic (Brass and Burkhardt 1993). Consistent with this premise, we complement the idea that networks are prisms that project structural signals by arguing that networks can also project behavioral signals based on networking interactions. In our network context, we suggest that the way traders interact with one another constitutes a behavioral signal that is important for assessing their character. In particular, when networking interactions are publicly visible, they can provide signals to all members of the network about an actor’s motives and intentions toward others, which serve as the basis for making generalized assessments of benevolence and goodwill. Such assessments translate into perceived trustworthiness by multiple traders toward a focal trader who, as a consequence, accumulates trust. A foundational feature of network interactions is the way in which actors communicate with one another (Monge and Contractor 2001). Whereas networking behavior has been often conceived of as purposive actions aimed toward tie formation (Forret and Dougherty 2001,Ingram and Morris 2007), we focus on the content of the communications. Specifically, we theorize that publicly visible content provides signals to all members of the network about a focal trader’s character and goodwill toward other traders with whom the focal trader interacts. A critical element of this networking behavior is the positive sentiments inherent in the communication content (Liu 2010). By positive sentiments, we mean “affective appraisals towards a person, such as liking someone, which represents the emotional potential in the relationship (Ridgeway 1994)” (Bianchi and Lancianese 2007, p. 7). In turn, the positive sentiments that traders perceive in the content of the focal trader’s communication provide an indication of the character and goodwill of the focal trader. For example, traders may signal their goodwill in communications when they convey a sense of responsibility, care, and concern for the well-being of others (seeTable 1). Importantly, for our purposes, the signal is meaningful not only for the recipient of the communication, but also for other traders observing it. To reiterate, our prismatic theory of trust suggests that it is the publicly available affective signal manifested in communications that creates the trusting decision on the part of the trustor. Consequently, to the extent that the focal trader’s (trustee’s) public communications are characterized by positive sentiments, the focal trader accumulates trust from other traders. Based on these arguments, we predict a networking behavior effect on trust accumulation as follows. The greater the focal trader’s communication of positive sentiments, the greater the focal trader’s trust accumulation. The Interplay of Network Structure and Networking Behavior We further argue that structure (status) and action (networking behavior) combine to affect trust accumulation. In particular, the benevolence indicated by positive sentiments reinforce the competence effect on trust accumulation signaled by a superior position in the social ordering (i.e., status). To the extent that trust arises from both competence and benevolence (Mayer et al. 1995), the co-occurrence of the two dimensions represents orthogonal components of trust. In our setting, when status is seen to be more authentically obtained, the positive sentiments expressed by high-status actors are seen as more credible than those by either low-status actors or actors whose high status has been inauthentically obtained (Hahl and Zuckerman 2014). Thus, when a high-status actor displays positivity in its interactions, its trustworthiness is enhanced beyond the effect of high status and positivity taken separately. Our argument further draws on the large literature that links the credibility of actors to their status. For example,Podolny (2010)shows that, when underlying quality is uncertain, status reduces the inherent uncertainty in the competence of the focal actor. Consequently, actions of high-status actors, including networking behaviors, are seen as more credible and consequential and take on an enhanced salience. In this vein,Sorenson and Stuart (2001)show that networking behaviors (e.g., endorsements) from high-status venture capitalists (VCs) are more valuable in helping ventures overcome market barriers to accessing funding. Relatedly,Stuart et al. (1999)find that endorsements from high-status VCs also lead to higher valued and quicker IPOs. Furthermore,Higgins and Gulati (2003)demonstrate that the greater legitimacy of endorsements from upper echelon members of banks enhances the likelihood of IPO success. We extend this line of research by theorizing the effect of communication, rather than endorsement, behavior. We theorize that, for high-status traders, the reputational consequences stemming from positive sentiments are greater than those of low-status traders. When traders are competent and also willing to share their insights with other traders, they are viewed as especially trustworthy because they are, in effect, helping other traders to become more competent and successful themselves. To the extent that positive sentiments convey the expert judgments of the focal trader and reveal how they make sense of uncertain, complex markets, the effect of their competence on perceived trustworthiness is strengthened. Thus, positive sentiments when projected by a high-status actor are more likely to be interpreted as signals of trustworthiness, resulting in greater trust accumulation. These arguments are summarized in our final hypothesis predicting the effect of the interplay of network structure and networking behavior on trust accumulation as follows. The effect of the focal trader’s status in the information-seeking network on the focal trader’s trust accumulation is enhanced by the focal trader’s communication of positive sentiments.
article.Methods: Data We test our predictions with data we collected on all traders on the EZ-Trade platform for 38 weeks, starting in 2012 and ending in 2013. Our data include information on all traders’ copy trading ties, information-seeking ties, communication content, and financial performance. There were more than 28,000 traders active on the platform during the period of the study. Not all traders were active (i.e., following, copying, or trading), however, throughout the entire time period.6This resulted in a panel of roughly 680,000 trader-by-week observations. Dependent Variable Trust accumulation (log) is the number of traders who copy trade a focal trader. In other words, it is the trust that a focal trader accumulates from other members of the network. Recall that another trader automatically copying a copied trader’s investments is the quintessential case of trust. Therefore, we count the number of traders that copy trade a focal trader at least once during the focal week.7The distribution of traders that copy trade another trader across time resembles a power law, in which most traders have close to zero copiers and a few outliers have a large number of copiers (the maximum we observe is 2,369 copiers). Such a distribution is expected and observed across many social networks (Barabási and Albert 1999). To account for this extreme skewness, we add one to each trader’s count of copiers in a given week and then take the natural log of the value to operationalizeTrust accumulation. Independent Variables Status (log). Actors are considered competent when they are of high status—specifically, when they are followed by other traders who are themselves highly followed (i.e., a focal trader with high eigenvector centrality).8Thus, to measure status, we calculate eigenvector centrality in the information-seeking (following) network. Eigenvector centrality is a widely used measure of status in social networks (e.g.,Bonacich 1987,Podolny 2001,Sorenson and Stuart 2001) that, in our case, captures the number of followers weighted by their number of followers and is calculated as follows: Positive Sentiments (log). To measure positive sentiments, we focus on communications initiated by the trustee on the discussion board of another trader. Because starting new communications involves initiating interactions with other traders rather than merely participating in communications started by others, we take the former to represent intentional networking behavior. In order to measure networking behavior expressing positive sentiments, we conduct a sentiment analysis of the text using the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) software package (Pennebaker et al. 2001).9LIWC is widely utilized in the organizational literature for text analysis (e.g.,Crilly et al. 2012,Kanze et al. 2018,Gamache and McNamara 2019). The software contains a validated dictionary of words that are associated with particular psychological states (e.g., positive emotion) and calculates the proportion of the body text that represents the particular psychological state. Representative words in the positive sentiments category include “appreciate,” “optimism,” and “supportive.” InTable 1, we present examples of texts from our sample that are identified as expressing positive sentiments by virtue of containing an above-average proportion of words that indicate positive sentiments. We calculate the average proportion of positive sentiments in all instances of the texts of interest identified above and find that the average text has 7.69% positive words. Next, consistent with prior research, we count the number of communications by the focal trader that contain a higher than average portion of positive sentiments (e.g.,Gamache and McNamara 2019). We find that this count variable is also power-law distributed and, therefore, add one and take the natural log of the count to arrive at the variablePositive sentiments.We lag this variable by one week in the models because it likely takes some time for traders to interpret observations of positive sentiments and assess their copying decisions on the platform.10 Control Variables We include several financial, network structure, and networking behavior controls that may be associated withTrust accumulationin meaningful ways. For consistency, we take the natural log of all nondummy control variables. Financial Controls. A trader who earns larger profits compared with other traders on the platform may accumulate the trust of more traders. Therefore, we includePerformance rankingas a control.Performance rankingis a variable that indicates the decile ranking of a trader’s performance in the previous week compared with other traders.High risk tradescaptures the proportion of trades carried out by the focal trader each week that are deemed by EZ-Trade’s in-house algorithm to be of high risk. Network Structure Controls. We control forFollowing and copying network overlap.Because we are focusing on trust accumulation in a network, we ensure that we control for the trust that a focal trader may accumulate from direct interactions. To compute this measure, we count the number of copiers in the current period who were followers of the focal trader in the prior week. We then divide this number by the total number of followers the focal trader had in the previous period. Furthermore, we includeCommunication and copying network overlap, which controls for the direct interactions between the focal trader and other traders in the form of communications posted on each other’s boards. To calculate this variable, we count the number of traders who initiated or received initial communication posts from the copied trader (trustee) and became copiers (trustors) in the next period as a proportion of the total number of traders with whom the copied trader exchanged such communication posts. Networking Behavior Controls. We control for features of the platform that may encourage traders to engage in more or less networking behavior. EZ-Trade monetarily compensates traders for accruing copy traders and the total amount of money that the copying traders are allocating to the copied trader. The incentive structure is tiered at different levels with compensation beginning when traders accrue at least 10 copiers. Therefore, traders may engage in more networking behavior for the sake of monetary compensation rather than to accumulate trust. We control for this possibility by including aNetworking incentive dummy, which is a dummy variable equal to one if the number of copiers of a focal trader is within one of the significant threshold of 10 (e.g., between 9 and 11 copiers). In the robustness checks section, we further considered the sensitivity of the results to variations in the threshold. In addition to the positive sentiments in the initial communications, the amount of initial communications and responses a focal trader posts may affect its visibility in the network. Therefore, we control forNumber of communications to others.In addition, posting communications on the focal trader’s own board may boost visibility as well, potentially eliciting trust of other traders. Thus, we control forNumber of communications to self.Finally, we also control for the general amount of attention that the focal trader receives from other traders and includeNumber of communications receivedas a control variable. We lag these variables by one week. Estimation Strategy We employ ordinary least square (OLS) regression with two-way (week and trader) fixed effects to explore the relationship between the trustee’s network position and networking behavior and trust accumulation. With week fixed effects, we are able to control for any factors that vary by week and that influence all traders equally (e.g., uncertainty in the financial markets). With trader fixed effects, we are able to control for any trader characteristics that are invariant over time, such as their country of origin, and may influence how likely others are to trust the focal trader. Our models take the form, for each traderiin each weekt, of
article.Results: Table 2presents the summary statistics for all variables. As expected,StatusandPositive sentimentsare positively correlated withTrust accumulation. This provides preliminary support for our prediction that the social structure surrounding trustees as well as their networking behavior are both positively related to trust accumulation. In addition, we observe the negative association betweenHigh risk tradesandTrust accumulation,which is also expected given that EZ-Trade formally dissuades traders from copying other traders who take excessive risks. Table 2.Descriptive Statistics and Correlations Table 2.Descriptive Statistics and Correlations Table 3presents the results of the OLS regression estimating the association between the trustee’s status and networking behavior on their trust accumulation. Model 1 includes the controls. Importantly, we find thatFollowing and copying network overlapandCommunication and copying network overlaphave positive and significant coefficients in all models, indicating that direct interactions between the trustee and other members of the network do contribute toTrust accumulation. In addition,Performance rankingof the focal trader is positive and significant in all the models. Table 3.OLS Regression Results for Trust Accumulation (Main Analysis) Table 3.OLS Regression Results for Trust Accumulation (Main Analysis) Note.For all models, cluster-robust standard errors (on trader) in parentheses. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. InHypothesis 1, we propose that higher levels ofStatusare associated with greaterTrust accumulationin the focal trader. In both Models 2 and 4, the coefficient forStatusis positive and significant (e.g.,𝛽β= 0.0002,p< 0.01 in Model 4). In particular, in Model 4, when the focal trader’s status increases from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile, the predicted value ofTrust accumulationincreases by 0.0094, which is about 16.20% of the average value ofTrust accumulation.These patterns provide support forHypothesis 1. InHypothesis 2, we predict that higher levels ofPositive sentimentsexpressed by the focal trader are associated with greaterTrust accumulationin the trader. In both Models 3 and 4, the coefficient forPositive sentimentsis positive and significant (e.g.,𝛽β= 0.1244,p< 0.01 in Model 4). Specifically in Model 4, when a trader’sPositive sentiments(number of positive posts) increases from zero to the mean number of positive posts by traders who post at least one positive post (approximately three posts),11the predicted value ofTrust accumulationincreases by 0.0498, which is about 85.51% of the average value ofTrust accumulation.These results are supportive of the prediction made inHypothesis 2. InHypothesis 3, we propose that the positive association betweenStatusandTrust accumulationis strengthened by thePositive sentimentsof the trustee. The coefficient of the interaction term betweenStatusandPositive sentimentsis positive and significant (e.g.,𝛽β= 0.0030,p< 0.05 in Model 4). To better understand the interaction effects ofStatusandPositive sentimentsonTrust accumulation, we plot the marginal effects ofTable 3, Model 4, using Stata 16’s “Margins” command. The results are shown inFigure 1. The figure plots the predicted value ofTrust accumulationacross two values ofStatus(25th and 75th percentile) and two values ofPositive sentiments(zero positive posts and the mean number of positive posts excluding zero posts). We find that, at the lower level ofStatus(25th percentile), the confidence intervals of the two lines overlap, indicating that the marginal effect ofStatusonTrust accumulationat the lower level ofPositive sentimentsis not significantly different from the marginal effect ofStatusonTrust accumulationat the higher level ofPositive sentiments. However, at the higher level ofStatus(75th percentile), the confidence intervals of the two lines do not overlap, indicating that the marginal effect ofStatusonTrust accumulationat the lower level ofPositive sentimentsis significantly different from the marginal effect ofStatusonTrust accumulationat the higher level ofPositive sentiments. More specifically, when a trader’sStatusandPositive sentimentsincrease from the 25th percentile to the 75th percentile, the predicted value ofTrust accumulationincreases by 0.1231, which is about 211% of the mean value ofTrust accumulation. The magnitude of the interaction effect onTrust accumulationis substantially larger than the effect of an increase ofPerformance rankingfrom the 25th to the 75th percentile, which is associated with a 2% increase inTrust accumulationfrom its mean. The substantial difference in the magnitude of the interaction effect compared with the effect of financial performance or evenStatusandPositive sentimentson their own provide strong support for effect of the interplay betweenStatusandPositive sentimentswe predicted inHypothesis 3. Supplementary Analyses Eigenvector vs. Degree Centrality. Whereas prior research suggests that both degree (in our case, the number of followers) and eigenvector (in our case, the number of followers weighted by their number of followers) centrality are measures of network prominence (Wasserman and Faust 1994,Bonacich 2007), their underlying mechanisms are different. In our context, given the dependent variable of trust accumulation, we argue that status, measured by eigenvector centrality, is a more appropriate and theoretically meaningful way to capture judgments of trustworthiness in a network (Podolny 2010) than degree centrality. In fact, the theoretical association between status and competence (a key dimension of trustworthiness) is well established in prior literature. Thus, a focal trader who is highly followed by traders who are themselves highly followed (i.e., a focal trader with high eigenvector centrality) is likely a better signal of competence because of its ability to capture status than a focal trader who is followed by a large number of novice traders (i.e., a focal trader with high degree centrality). The logic here is analogous to relying on the Michelin guide, which is based on expert assessments, for restaurant recommendations (Rao et al. 2005) rather than Google reviews, which are accumulated from amateur diners among the general public. In this vein, asHubbard et al. (2018, p. 1978) posit, “The sociocognitive content of status draws attention to relationships and relative social standing, emphasizing that high-status actors’ merits [knowledge and competence] have been vetted by others.” In order to validate the appropriateness of using eigenvector centrality as opposed to the alternative of degree centrality in the information-seeking (following) network as a measure of competence, we conducted an experiment to ascertain the relative strength of each of these measures for capturing competence (please see Online Appendix B for details). We found that participants were more likely to seek information from the trader with higher status (eigenvector centrality) compared with the trader with higher popularity (degree centrality). Likewise, we found that participants evaluated the trading competence of the higher status trader as greater than that of the trader with higher popularity. As further validation of the appropriateness of using eigenvector centrality as opposed to degree centrality, we empirically compared the relative effects of the two variables entered in the same model. Such an estimation is challenging not only because of collinearity, but also the fact that the two variables are highly intertwined both theoretically and empirically (Bonacich 2007). Accordingly, we conduct a residualized relative importance analysis (LeBreton et al. 2013,Vardaman et al. 2015), followingZaheer and Bell (2005), to partial out the influence of degree on eigenvector centrality. Our results show that, whereas both degree and eigenvector centrality are positively and significantly related to trust accumulation, eigenvector centrality (status) has a significantly larger effect than degree centrality (F1, 25,325= 38.18,p< 0.01) (see Online Table C.1). To confirm that the previous analysis yields results consistent with different procedures designed to estimate the relative effects of two variables that are highly correlated and fundamentally intertwined, we followZhang et al. (2017), who disentangle the independent effects of degree and eigenvector centrality by using an orthogonalization procedure (Golub and Van Loan 2013). Using this procedure, we find results that are consistent with the results of the residualized procedure (see Online Table C.2). Effects of Time-Varying Unobservables. Even though we include week and trader fixed effects, there still may be unobservable time-varying factors that influence the degree to which a trader is able to accumulate trust across the network. For example, certain financial products may change in their popularity over time. This implies that traders on the platform may look to trust traders with specific product competencies at different points in time. Alternatively, capabilities of the focal trader may change as time goes on. This implies that time-varying unobservable characteristics may be confounds, and as a result, our estimates based on OLS may be biased. In order to determine if such effects are likely to be biasing our results, we employ the Arellano–Bond generalized method of moments system estimator for dynamic panel models (Arellano and Bond 1991,Arellano and Bover 1995,Blundell and Bond 1998) as per recent network studies (e.g.,Vandaie and Zaheer 2015,Clement et al. 2018). The results of these analyses, presented inTable 4, confirm thatStatus,Positive sentiments, and their interaction all have positive and significant coefficients, consistent with our OLS findings inTable 3. These results suggest that, even when accounting for time-varying factors explicitly, the relationship betweenStatus,Positive sentiments, and the interaction of the two have robust positive associations withTrust accumulation, providing further support for our main arguments. Table 4.Dynamic Panel Arellano-Bond Results for Trust Accumulation (Supplementary Analysis) Table 4.Dynamic Panel Arellano-Bond Results for Trust Accumulation (Supplementary Analysis) Note.For all models, robust standard errors in parentheses. *p< 0.1; **p< 0.05; ***p< 0.01. Robustness Checks We further investigated the possibility of several confounding effects that may alter our results. The results of the analyses described below are available upon request. Incentive Effects. We consider the possibility that the incentive for networking behavior provided on the platform may influence the amount of trust a trader accumulates in two alternative ways. First, we consider the possibility that traders may be more sensitive to the incentive structure. In particular, a trader may adjust its behavior not only when the trader is just one copying trader away from the critical threshold of 10 (e.g., between 9 and 11 copiers) as we have assumed. Therefore, in additional analyses, we adjust theNetworking incentive dummyto take on the value of one when the trader is within two copiers of 10 (e.g., between 8 and 12 copiers). Second, we consider the possibility that traders are influenced by higher tiered monetary incentives rather than the initial critical level of 10 copiers. Therefore, beyond the initial critical level of 10, traders may change their networking behavior when they are close to the next critical threshold of 50 copiers. Thus, we alter theNetworking incentive dummyto equal one when the trader is within one copier of 50 (e.g., between 49 and 51 copiers). Across all these alternative specifications, we find results consistent with those reported inTable 3. General Likability. In order to better isolate benevolence and goodwill in the networking behavior of the focal trader, we also considered whether the networking behavior effects occurred because of the general likability of the focal trader (Stavrova et al. 2022). To tease apart the differential effect of general likability from positivity directed at other traders, we created a variable that captures the positive sentiments expressed in the traders’ posts on their own boards. When we include this variable in our main models, we do find a positive and significant main effect, whereas its interaction with status is not statistically significant. More importantly, our main results remain unchanged. Indirect Connections. To explicitly account for indirect connections among traders that may play a role in the accumulation of trust, we calculated two new variables,Following network indirectandCommunication network indirect, that use geodesic distance between trustors and trustees to capture indirect pathways to trust formation. We find that the two variables have positive and significant effects on trust accumulation. At the same time, our main results hold.
article.Discussion: To what extent does trust in the context of social networks occur in the absence of connections? The apparent oxymoron is the premise and promise of network forms of trust—a potentially new class of trust concepts (McEvily et al. 2021). Moving from a theoretical possibility to proof of concept is the challenge we take up in this paper. In particular, we claim that prismatic trust emerges in social structures even in the absence of direct and indirect experience among actors. Specifically, we study the emergence of trust in a context in which network positions and networking behaviors are publicly observable. The results strongly support our contention that both status and networking behavior, as well as their interplay, explain variation in trust accumulation in a network actor. Critically, the influence of prismatic signals on trust persists over and above any reputational effects from performance (Ertug and Castellucci 2013,Diekmann et al. 2014). We believe our study offers encouraging pioneering evidence to substantiate the existence and role of prismatic trust. Our point of departure fromMcEvily et al. (2021)is shifting from the potential for network trust (“prototrust”) to identifying, operationalizing, and measuring the conditions giving rise to the actual placement of trust in network settings. At the same time, our conceptualization of prismatic trust is fundamentally distinct from existing pipes and pathways notions of trust in networks. Whereas prior research has conceptualized trust as content flowing through network ties based on diffusion processes (Wong and Boh 2010), our notion of prismatic trust is not based on direct or even indirect pathways among network actors. Rather, we claim that networks also produce a different form of trust: one that is generated by both the prismatic status effect of network structure and importantly includes a new class of network mechanisms in the sense of behaviors publicly displayed on the network stage. In this regard, we go beyond structural prismatic status effects by also capturing networking behavior (expressing positive sentiments) as enacted through performances by which actors demonstrate their benevolent character in the course of interacting with other network members. We are unaware of other research that has linked prismatic effects, both structural and behavioral, to the creation of trust in networks, and in this respect, we believe that our concept of prismatic trust is inherently novel relative to other well-studied forms of trust. We see several important theoretical and empirical implications flowing from our view of prismatic trust. First, by combining both the structural prismatic status effect and the behavioral prismatic sentiment effect, we are able to theoretically integrate the complementarity between structure and action (Giddens 1979). Whereas the role of action has been underemphasized in prior network literature, we advance our understanding of the combination of structure and behavior in networks by identifying and theorizing the specific role of positive sentiments as a form of networking behavior. As our results suggest, valued network outcomes are not solely the result of occupying preferential positions in social structure, but also derive from the deliberate and intentional actions undertaken by actors in the course of interacting with other network members. It is noteworthy that relatively few instances of the expression of positive sentiments generate large prismatic trust effects, particularly for high-status actors. In the well-known interaction between structure and action (e.g.Soda et al. 2018), it appears that rare behavioral events have an outsized impact. To the extent that network status is largely outside the control of individual actors, positive sentiments may be a relatively more accessible way for actors to accumulate trust. At the same time, the interplay of structure and action is what has the greatest impact on trust accumulation. A second important insight emerging from our research is that it speaks directly to outcomes emanating from the evolution of personal and professional networks into digital spaces. In such network settings, information is much more publicly available and ubiquitous, suggesting the relevance of the observability of both network structures and networking behaviors as trust triggers. As the progression from traditionally off-line and physical interactions to those that are partially or completely online and virtual continues, the observability condition in networks becomes a more ubiquitous, salient, and consequential feature of the fabric of everyday social interactions. Our theory and empirics emphasize the critical role public information plays in the formation of trust, which represents a sharp contrast with the emphasis of organizational research on trust. Specifically, previous research on relational trust (Mayer and Davis 1999) and embeddedness (Uzzi 1996) focuses on information that is private—in the sense of being exclusive to the two members of a dyad—to explain trust emergence. Conversely, the role of public information available to any member of the network in the creation of trust has received much less research attention. The current study allows us to appreciate the potential for trust to occur in network spaces that are public or quasi-public. Third, we see prismatic trust as raising an intriguing possibility about the fungibility of trust as a resource. Whereas relational forms of trust are characterized by an intrinsic social budget (Hill and Dunbar 2003) that necessarily circumscribes trust to a finite set of relationships, network forms of trust generated by public signals are limited only by the size of the network. As a result, vertical differentiation in this kind of online social structure can be much starker than in other kinds of social structures such as physical ones. Paradoxically, a system that is designed to be democratic in the sense that any member of the network can connect to any other member, thus, becomes massively scalable and even elitist in terms of a power-law distribution: a small number of actors accumulating a disproportionate share of trust. At the system level, given the concentration of trust as a resource in a small number of actors, such distribution heightens dependence and, therefore, distills influence and power on a relatively exclusive set of actors (Casciaro and Piskorski 2005,Gulati and Sytch 2007). Fourth, with the concept of prismatic trust, we extend trust theory by introducing a new mechanism underlying trust characterized by a distinctive ontology. Our theory of prismatic trust mirrors the evolutionary trajectory of contemporary society in which online networks play a fundamental role in shaping individual and collective behaviors. With prismatic trust, we broaden the concept of trust to spell out its ontology and mechanisms in a digital, networked, and online society. Finally, our research underscores the long-held but rarely studied notion that trust is a foundational logic of networks (Granovetter 1985,Coleman 1988,Powell 1990). A striking feature of our findings is that it is not simply financial performance that informs decisions and behaviors in the context of the economically salient market-based social trading platform we study. Rather, the network form of trust we identify also indicates with whom traders are prepared to take a leap of faith when it comes to trading decisions. In this way, we further enhance our understanding of the social structure of financial markets (Baker 1984) by also unearthing the networking behaviors underlying and shaping network structure (Abolafia and Kilduff 1988). Our theory of prismatic trust is also strengthened by our research design, which considers multiple networks created by different types of ties as drivers of trust formation: trustors observing, trustees communicating, and network members following. As a result, our findings are likely to be robust to the endogeneity problems arising from conventional studies in which both network antecedents and outcomes are driven by the same strategic actor and studied within the same network. Furthermore, our empirical strategy is a consequence of studying forms of trust that arise in the absence of direct or indirect connections, which necessarily introduces actors unrelated to the trustor and the trustee making trust judgments. By doing so, we are able to show that even strangers who have no experience with focal network actors may trust them because of their status and networking behavior. Limitations and Future Research Directions In addition to the contributions to the literature, our research also raises important questions for further consideration. We recognize that the publicness of status signals and networking behaviors varies from one network context to another. The features that are available on EZ-Trade may not be available in other network settings. For example, in online networks such as ours, observable signals of status from network structure and of positivity from networking behavior serve as boundary conditions. When network interactions are less public—for example, direct messaging, healthcare, dating services—our prismatic theory of trust may be driven by somewhat different mechanisms. At the same time, our theory of prismatic trust holds great potential to complement the burgeoning literature on online communities (e.g.,Hampton and Wellman 2001,Hampton 2016,Smirnova et al. 2022). We also recognize that potential biases arising from observability, such as gender- and race-based discrimination (Fernandez and Fernandez-Mateo 2006,Fernandez-Mateo and Kaplan 2018) may occur by virtue of the public observability of actors. Understanding the extent to which observability of network interactions generates such forms of inequality is an important priority for future research. Whereas observability is a common feature of online communities, we suggest that our theory of prismatic trust is not limited to online network settings. In particular, given the prevalence of hybrid network settings, in which online interactions extend off line (and vice versa), the mechanisms of trust (prismatic, pathway, relational, institutional) are likely to spill over from one setting to another. For example, in the case of prismatic trust, the observability that informs trust judgments online also carries over off line such that other trust mechanisms may come into play (as complements, substitutes, or triggers). Whereas the question of how multiple trust mechanism combine and co-occur is beyond the scope of the present study, we see it as a fertile area for future research. Our research is expressly predicated on explaining trust accumulation outcomes in the copying network from status in the information-seeking network and networking behaviors based on communications. The logic we used is aimed at avoiding the potential circularity in explaining both antecedents and outcomes from the same network. At the same time, there may be interesting future research directions in understanding whether and how symmetric antecedents or outcomes might affect one another, such as the extent to which decisions to follow a specific trader translate into decisions to copy the trader. Future research may also examine the durability of trust in network settings given that trust is known to create stickiness in relationships. In our EZ-Trade context, we find that the average copying relationship lasts nearly four weeks, which is remarkable given that exit is practically costless. Rather than taking as given the stickiness of trust, future research could assess the determinants of trust durability under different network conditions. Another potential direction for future research is the issue of network architects (McEvily and Zaheer 2004,Provan and Kenis 2008) and the role that such actors play in designing and overseeing the governance of the network. Although the EZ-Trade platform was created and is managed by a network architect, our setting does not lend itself to exploring the many interesting questions related to varied forms of network governance and its trustworthiness. By the same token, we see such questions as important avenues for subsequent examination.
article.Conclusion: Our exploration of prismatic trust considers a trust form situated in the void between the more extensively studied dyadic and institutional forms and strengthens our conviction that this is a novel mechanism of trust that operates according to a distinctive logic. As a result, we see instances of trust that cross time, space, and networks in ways that we did not see before. Focusing scholarly attention on prismatic trust is important because it permits the exploration of novel questions and phenomena. In particular, prismatic trust enables the identification and study of trust that is massively scalable, extending beyond just an actor’s capacity for direct or even indirect experiences with other actors. The scalability of trust in network settings implies that the greater efficiency of trusting judgments can lubricate and vastly expand organizational, economic, and social activity. As actors become increasingly interconnected across contexts, opportunities for interaction greatly exceed actors’ cognitive and social capacities. In order to take advantage of expanded opportunities, actors require a different, more efficient, modality of trust production than is possible with interpersonal and dyadic trust. From a managerial and organizational standpoint, our research underscores the critical role of the observability of network signals as a foundational mechanism of trust in an increasingly networked world. Thus, the scale of trust at a collective level—for example, organizations, communities, platforms, ecosystems—hinges on the balance between what is private and what is public. In addition to promoting interpersonal trust, leaders and managers should consider the opportunities for designing public spaces in which signals of trust are projected to a broader audience. Our concept of prismatic trust extends trust theory by introducing a new mechanism of trust characterized by a distinctive ontology and offers a parallel to the evolutionary trajectory of modern society in which online networks play a fundamental role in shaping individual and collective behaviors.
